exemplar.title: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Maintains a sustained focus on the prescribed title, with clear definitions of ârevered knowledgeâ and âfragility.â
Effectively structured comparison between the arts (cinema) and history, anchored by relevant examples spanning early to contemporary film and the Hiroshima/Nagasaki case.
Clear thesis and coherent organization, with smooth transitions signaling shifts between areas of knowledge.
Arguments are supported by specific examples and theoretical references (Bazinâs realism, Benjaminâs aura, Carrâs interpretivism).
Integrative synthesis that connects insights from both AOKs and considers the implications of fragility and resilience across domains.
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive, with limited probing of underlying knowledge questions and minimal development of TOK concepts such as justification and perspective.
Breadth of AOK treatment is uneven: the arts discussion is confined almost entirely to cinema, and history centers on a single case study without broader historiographical perspectives.
Evaluation of counterclaims and alternative viewpoints is thin; the essay mentions shifts in moral frameworks and global dissent but does not rigorously test these against evidence.
Key TOK concepts are stated but not analysed in depth or linked explicitly to knowledge frameworks (e.g., communal validation, criteria for reliability).
Conclusion reiterates earlier claims without advancing reflective evaluation or posing new knowledge questions, reducing the overall evaluative rigour.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Given the official total mark of 8 and the evidence from the strand assessment, the essay demonstrates many of the characteristics of the 7â8 band: a sustained focus on the title, clear and coherent arguments supported by specific examples, and some evaluation of different perspectives. However, it falls short of the highest band due to its largely descriptive treatment, uneven breadth, and limited depth of TOK conceptual analysis. Awarding 8 marks reflects the essayâs solid performance in structure, focus, and use of examples while acknowledging the need for greater evaluative rigour and more comprehensive integration of TOK concepts.

annotations.comment: The conclusion reiterates earlier claims without advancing the argument. The student should offer a reflective evaluation of the broader significance of knowledge fragility for understanding what counts as reliable knowledge.

The student makes a broad knowledge claim about evolving perspectives but does not specify criteria for assessing reliability. Including explicit evaluation metrics would enhance the rigor of the conclusion.

The student defines ârevered knowledgeâ and âfragilityâ but these definitions remain abstract and unconnected to specific TOK terminology or knowledge frameworks. They should refine these definitions by linking them to knowledge questions and core concepts like justification and perspective to ground the discussion.

The student articulates a clear thesis outlining the comparative approach between the arts and history, establishing a coherent structure for the essay. This focused framework provides a solid foundation for developing arguments in each area of knowledge.

While the exploration of film techniques in the arts is explicit, the discussion narrowly confines âthe artsâ to cinema. To strengthen breadth, the student should include other art forms or explain why cinema exemplifies broader artistic resilience more comprehensively.

The analysis of the wide shot remains largely descriptive, recounting its historical use rather than critically examining how its adaptability evidences the fragility or resilience of knowledge. The student should probe how this exemplifies shifting artistic values and assumptions.

The student effectively selects examples spanning early (LumiÃ¨re brothers) to contemporary (Nolan, Kubrick) cinema to illustrate the enduring relevance of the wide shot. This diachronic range grounds the argument in concrete case studies.

Benjaminâs 1935 thesis is applied without addressing its contemporary relevance. The student should contextualize how Benjaminâs ideas resonate with 21st-century digital practices to demonstrate ongoing fragility in film knowledge.
The transition to the history AOK is clearly signaled, ensuring a well-structured comparison. This shift maintains coherence and focus on the prescribed titleâs interdisciplinary requirements.
Identifying utilitarianism as the moral theory underpinning American narratives on Hiroshima and Nagasaki demonstrates an understanding of how ethical frameworks shape historical knowledge.

The analysis describes the utilitarian perspective but stops short of interrogating its limitations or counter-claims. The student should evaluate alternative moral theories and their implications for the stability of historical narratives.
Linking the global response to Hiroshima and Nagasaki to the Non-Proliferation Treaty effectively illustrates how shared moral consensus can confer resilience on certain historical knowledge.

The assertion of global unanimity overlooks political dissent and treaty loopholes. Evaluating these counterpoints would offer a more nuanced view of the fragility or resilience of collective historical memory.

The student synthesizes insights from arts and history in the implications paragraph, demonstrating an integrative understanding of how knowledge in different domains can share similar vulnerabilities.

The stated implications remain descriptive rather than evaluative. To deepen TOK analysis, the student should pose explicit knowledge questions emerging from this synthesis and explore their ramifications.

The citation of James Learoyd (2020) supporting Bazinâs view lacks evaluation of source credibility. The student should assess the authority of this reference and explain its relevance to the TOK discussion of knowledge reliability.

The claim that cinematographic techniques are âadaptiveâ would benefit from a clearer explanation of the mechanisms by which film knowledge evolves. Consider linking this to theories of knowledge acquisition and communal validation.

Incorporating AndrÃ© Bazinâs concept of objective reality highlights theoretical underpinnings for the wide shotâs resilience. This connection to film theory enriches the discussion of enduring knowledge in the arts.

Introducing Walter Benjaminâs theory on the âauraâ connects technological change to shifts in artistic methodology, effectively situating the fragility of aesthetic knowledge within a broader philosophical framework.

The student invokes Benjaminâs aura concept but does not explicitly link it back to the fragility of set design or practical effects. They should bridge this theoretical insight with specific examples to clarify its TOK implication.

The discussion of practical effects transitions abruptly and the sentence trails off, undermining coherence. The student should complete the thought on how CGI supersedes traditional methods to reinforce the argument on knowledge fragility.

Relying solely on Star Wars and Avatar examples limits the analysis of technological shifts in film. To deepen evaluation, incorporate contrasting examples where practical effects have been revived or revalued in modern cinema.

The focus on American justification neglects Japanese or global historiographical perspectives. Incorporating diverse viewpoints would strengthen awareness of how multiple narratives impact the fragility of revered history.
Using E.H. Carrâs assertion that history is interpretative situates the discussion within core TOK debates on perspective and the constructed nature of knowledge.

Claims about shifting utilitarian views among newer American generations lack empirical evidence. The student should cite sociological or historiographical studies to substantiate the evolution of moral perspectives.

The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
